Scenario.overview: Energy Complex A sophisticated attack targeting an energy sector organization using the Triton malware to manipulate safety systems, beginning with spear-phishing and escalating to physical infrastructure disruption.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Sabotage industrial control systems to cause operational disruption

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: Adversaries gathered public information about the target’s infrastructure, including WHOIS records and contact details, to identify potential vulnerabilities in non-production interfaces exposed on production systems.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: A domain mimicking the target’s official website was registered to host phishing content, enabling adversaries to distribute malicious payloads disguised as legitimate communications.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Victims received tailored phishing emails containing weaponized RTF/DOCX attachments. These exploited unpatched software vulnerabilities to establish initial foothold on the network.

stage.name: Execution
stage.technique_name: Exploit Vulnerability in Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP)
stage.capec: CAPEC-555: Remote Services with Stolen Credentials
stage.description: Adversaries leveraged unpatched RDP flaws to execute arbitrary code on compromised systems, establishing persistence and expanding access to internal networks.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: A persistent backdoor was deployed via Windows Task Scheduler, allowing attackers to maintain long-term access to the network despite temporary disruptions.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Weakened service account permissions were exploited to escalate privileges, granting attackers administrative access to critical systems.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious code was injected into legitimate processes (e.g., svchost.exe) to evade detection by endpoint security solutions.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-644: Use of Captured Hashes (Pass The Hash)
stage.description: Credentials were extracted from memory using tools like Mimikatz, enabling lateral movement across the network.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Internal network mapping identified critical assets, including PLCs and SCADA systems, to prioritize targeting for impact.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-555: Remote Services with Stolen Credentials
stage.description: Stolen credentials were used to brute-force RDP access, moving laterally toward industrial control systems.

stage.name: Collection
stage.description: Sensitive operational data, including SIS configurations, was harvested from memory dumps of compromised hosts.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Stolen data was exfiltrated via encrypted HTTPS channels or DNS tunneling to avoid detection by network monitoring systems.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Triton malware altered SIS logic, triggering unsafe conditions in industrial processes and risking physical damage or operational shutdowns.

Scenario.summary: A multi-stage attack targeting energy infrastructure began with spear-phishing, escalated through privilege escalation and lateral movement, and culminated in the manipulation of safety systems using Triton malware, demonstrating advanced adversarial capabilities.
